cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer. The question is about the 1974 World Bank study mentioned in the passage. The key point is what the study concluded regarding the benefits of economic growth in developing countries. First, I'll go back to the relevant part of the text. The passage says, "A major study by the World Bank in 1974 concluded that it is now clear that more than decades of rapid growth in developing countries has been of little benefit to a third of their population." So the study found that even though these countries had rapid growth, the benefit was only to a third of the population. That means the majority (two-thirds) didn't get much benefit. The question is asking what the economic growth brought to some backward countries (which I assume are the developing countries referred to here). The options are (A) benefit to a third, (B) two-thirds, (C) little benefit to their people, (D) no benefit at all. Looking at the conclusion, the study says "little benefit to a third." But the question is phrased as "brought _." The answer needs to reflect the overall effect. The study says the benefit was only to a third, so for the rest, there was little or no benefit. But the options are about the benefit brought by growth in these countries. The study is saying that the growth didn't result in benefits for most people. The answer might be (C) little benefit to their people, because if only a third had benefits, then most (two-thirds) didn't, meaning overall there was little benefit to "their people" (the population in general). Alternatively, (A) would be correct if the question is specifically about the third that benefited. But the way the question is phrased, "brought ___" without specifying to which part of the population, the answer should be the overall conclusion. Since the study concluded that growth had little benefit to a third, which implies that most people didn't benefit,
